coredata.dc:title: A label-free fluorescence nanosensor based on nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped carbon quantum dots for ultra-sensitive detection of new coccine in food samples
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Lizhen
Mi, Zhi
Huo, Xingyan
Yuan, Lin
Bao, Yayan
Liu, Zhixiong
Feng, Feng
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, an innovative method for the sensitive detection of new coccine using N, P-doped carbon quantum dots (N,P-CQDs) as fluorescent nanosensor is reported for the first time. The sensing mechanism is based on the fluorescence quenching of N,P-CQDs by new coccine through inner filter effect (IFE). N,P-CQDs were prepared by simple hydrothermal treatment of citric acid, phosphoric acid and ethylenediamine. Under the optimal conditions, the new coccine has two good linear responses in the concentration range of 0.2–100 and 100–200 μM, and the detection limits are as low as 24.8 and 9.4 nM, respectively. Our developed nanosensor has been successfully used for the determination of new coccine in food samples with good precision and high accuracy. This work highlights the economic, rapid, simple, selective and ultra-sensitive for new coccine detection, and opens up a new way for the monitoring of new coccine in actual food samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Fluorescence nanosensor
New coccine

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence response of the N,P-CQDs to new coccine
Determination of new coccine in actual food samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV–vis absorption (blue line), excitation (red line) and emission (black line) spectra of the N,P-CQDs solution. Inset: photograph of the N,P-CQDs solution under daylight lamp (left) and UV light lamp (right). (B) The fluorescence emission spectra of N,P-CQDs at different excitation wavelengths (300–450 nm). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(A) TEM image of N,P-CQDs and (B) size distribution of N,P-CQDs.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of N,P-CQDs at different new coccine concentrations (0–200 µM). (B) and (C) are the linear relationships between F0/F and the different concentrations of new coccine (0.2–100 µM and 100–200 µM). The error bars represent the standard deviation of three independent experiments.

Fluorescence response of N,P-CQDs in the presence of new coccine (60 μM), metal ions (1 M), sugars (1 M), vitamins (1 M) and preservatives (1 M). The error bars represent the standard deviation of three independent experiments.
The synthesis process of N,P-CQDs and their application to the determination of new coccine in Mirinda soft drink, candy, fruit jam and paprika samples.
Analysis of new coccine in food samples based on this work and HPLC (n = 5).

head.ce:title: A label-free fluorescence nanosensor based on nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped carbon quantum dots for ultra-sensitive detection of new coccine in food samples

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanxi Datong University, Datong 037009, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shanxi Datong University, Datong 037009, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanxi Normal University, Linfen 041004, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shanxi Normal University, Linfen 041004, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, an innovative method for the sensitive detection of new coccine using N, P-doped carbon quantum dots (N,P-CQDs) as fluorescent nanosensor is reported for the first time. The sensing mechanism is based on the fluorescence quenching of N,P-CQDs by new coccine through inner filter effect (IFE). N,P-CQDs were prepared by simple hydrothermal treatment of citric acid, phosphoric acid and ethylenediamine. Under the optimal conditions, the new coccine has two good linear responses in the concentration range of 0.2–100 and 100–200 μM, and the detection limits are as low as 24.8 and 9.4 nM, respectively. Our developed nanosensor has been successfully used for the determination of new coccine in food samples with good precision and high accuracy. This work highlights the economic, rapid, simple, selective and ultra-sensitive for new coccine detection, and opens up a new way for the monitoring of new coccine in actual food samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Food colors have been used to make foods more attractive and appetizing. In recent years, natural food colors have become more and more popular among consumers due to their high safety and no side effects (Wu, Pu, Huang, & Sun, 2020)). However, most natural food colors are expensive to extract, have poor stability, and are susceptible to light, heat and other chemical substances (Ou et al., 2018). On the contrary, synthetic food colors have the advantages of low price, rich color, strong coloring power, and not easy to fade, which are widespread used in the field of foods processing. However, excessive intake of synthetic food colors can cause harm to human health (Yao et al., 2018). Therefore, the type and amount of synthetic food colors in foods should be strictly controlled.

New coccine (also called as: E124; Ponceau 4R; Acid red 18; Cochineal red A) is a commonly used synthetic food colors, which belongs to strong acid dyes. The full chemical name is 1-(4-sulfonic acid-1-naphthalenazo)-2-hydroxy-6,8-naphthalene disulfonate sodium. Excessive new coccine has been shown to have severe carcinogenic and mutagenic effects (Tsuda et al., 2001). The United States, Canada, Denmark, Norway and many other countries prohibit the use of new coccine in any foods (Cyriac, Sivasankaran, & Kumar, 2018). In many countries, it is allowed as an additive and has a strict level of use in certain foods (such as beverages, candies and pickles) (Xie et al., 2012). In China, the use of new coccine in the food industry is limited to frozen drinks, jams, beverages, candies, cakes, syrups, preserves and cans, and the maximum allowable amount of new coccine in these foods is 0.05 to 0.5 g/kg (GB 2760-2014). The acceptable daily intake of new coccine for humans as established by Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO) and World Health Organization (WHO) Joint Expert Committee on Food Additives (JECFA, 2019) is 0–4 mg/kg body weight. However, there are still many reports about the excessive addition of new coccine in foods (Chen et al., 2017; Iammarino et al., 2020; Cyriac et al., 2018). In order to ensure the health of consumers, it is of utmost importance to establish a simple, rapid, sensitive and accurate method for effective detection of new coccine in foodstuffs.

At present, capillary electrophoresis (CE) (Ryvolové, Táborský, Vrýbel, Krásenský, & Preisler, 2007), high performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS) (Tsai, Kuo, & Shih, 2015), HPLC-ultra violet (HPLC-UV) (Dixit, Khanna, & Das, 2010; Khanavi et al., 2012), high performance ion chromatography (HPIC) (Chen, Mou, Hou, Riviello, & Ni, 1998) and surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) spectra (Xie et al., 2012; Wu, Pu, Huang, & Sun, 2020) have been proposed for new coccine detection. Nevertheless, the great majority of these methods are expensive, time-costing, complicated sample pretreatment and cumbersome operation. Therefore, more and more researches are devoting to the development of economical, fast, sensitive and effective technology for the detection of new coccine. Fluorescence technology has been widely used in chemical and biochemical analysis due to its economy, superior sensitivity, excellent accuracy, simple operation and fast response (Mohapatra, Sahu, Sinha, & Bhutia, 2015). For new coccine analysis, fluorescence method also has greater potential than other methods.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are one of the most popular nanomaterials in the photoluminescent carbon nano family with a size of less than 10 nm. CQDs have gain much attention due to their outstanding characteristics, such as good solubility in water, high chemical inertness, tunable emission, excellent photostability, large stokes shift, low cell toxicity and superior biocompatibility (Bao, Liu, Zhang, & Pang, 2015; Fang et al., 2017; Li et al., 2016a, 2016b). In view of the excellent characteristics of CQDs, CQDs display great application potential in fluorescence sensing. In particular, CQDs have excellent feature for easy functionalization, and can be further doped with heteroatoms to improve the detection sensitivity of CQDs, which was doped with phosphorus (P) (Gong et al., 2017), sulfur (S) (Qu, Zhang, Yang, Hu, & Qu, 2019), nitrogen (N) (Liu et al., 2019), chlorine (Cl) (Wang et al., 2018) and boron (B) (Jia et al., 2019), etc. Up to now, doped CQDs have been widely used in the detection of various substances, such as nitrite (Sun et al., 2021), azithromycin (Guo et al., 2021), Co2+ (Tian, Zhang, Liu, Wang, & Zhang, 2021), Ag+ (Huang, Yang, Yao, Liu, & Xiao, 2018), ʟ-cysteine (Huang, Yang, Yao, Liu, & Xiao, 2018) and Cl- (Li, & Xu, 2020), etc. However, no studies have reported the use of CQDs for the determination of new coccine.

In the present study, a N,P-CQDs-based fluorescent nanosensor was developed for the selective and sensitive determination of new coccine for the first time (Scheme 1 ). N,P-CQDs were synthesized by a straightforward one-step hydrothermal method with ethylenediamine, citric acid and phosphoric acid as precursors. The structural, optical and morphological properties of N,P-CQDs were characterized by fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), x-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), UV–vis spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy and transmission electron microscope (TEM). The prepared N,P-CQDs possess superior water-solubility and strong emission fluorescence. The fluorescence quantum yield is up to 43.2%. The fluorescence of N,P-CQDs can be selectively and sensitively quenched by new coccine. The optimal sensing conditions (the amount of N,P-CQDs, pH and incubation time) and quenching mechanism were studied in detail. In addition, the analytical performances (linearity, detection limit and selectivity) of the sensing system for new coccine detection were investigated. The sensing system achieves a detection limit as low as 9.4 nM, which is lower than previously established analytical methods (Tsai, Kuo, & Shih, 2015; Dixit, Khanna, & Das, 2010; Khanavi et al., 2012; Chen, Mou, Hou, Riviello, & Ni, 1998; Ryvolové, Táborský, Vrýbel, Krásenský, & Preisler, 2007; Xie et al., 2012; Wu, Pu, Huang, & Sun, 2020). It is sufficient to monitor new coccine in food samples and can be used to screen and control foods. Finally, the fluorescent nanosensor based on N,P-CQDs was utilized for the analysis of new coccine in food samples with good precision and high accuracy. As far as we aware, this is the first time report on using N,P-CQDs as a novel fluorescent nanosensor for the ultrasensitive detection of new coccine in food samples. Our proposed fluorescent nanosensor has the merits of simplicity, good selectivity, low cost, wide linear range, rapidity and high sensitivity, which opens up a new avenue for the monitoring of new coccine in foods.

Ethanediamine, quinine sulfate, citric acid, phosphoric acid (85 %) and new coccine were bought from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Corporation Limited (Shanghai, China). Sodium metaphosphate, sodium benzoate, β-cyclodextrin, Vitamin B1, Vitamin C, sucrose, glucose, lactose, methanol and ammonium acetate were obtained from Tianjin Zhiyuan Chemical Reagent Corporation Limited (Tianjin, China). KBr, NaH2PO4, NaOH, Na2HPO4, HCl, FeCl3, ZnCl2, MgCl2, AlCl3, KCl, BaCl2, CaCl2 and NaCl were bought from Tianjin Chemical Three Plant Corporation Limited (Tianjin, China). All chemicals are of analytical grade and used directly without any purification treatment. Deionized water (18.2 MΩ) obtained from a Millipore MilliQ System was used throughout this work. 10 mM phosphate buffer solution was used to adjust the pH value of the solution. The stock solution of 10 mM new coccine was prepared with deionized water.

The size and morphology of N,P-CQDs were obtained by transmission electron microscope (TEM) images at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV using a JEM-2011TEM (JEOL, Japan). Samples for TEM were prepared by adding a drop of N,P-CQDs solution to a 400 mesh Cu grid coated with Agar carbon. N,P-CQDs' optical properties were studied by fluorescence and UV–vis absorption spectra using a a F-2500 spectrofluorometer (Hitachi, Japan) and UV-2700 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan), respectively. The structure and surface chemical composition of N,P-CQDs were investigated by x-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) using an ESCALAB 250 x-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) and a Tensor 27 FT-IR spectrometer (Bruker, Germany), respectively. The FT-IR spectra of N,P-CQDs was recorded at a resolution of 4 cm−1 in the wavenumber range of 500–3500 cm−1. The fluorescence lifetime of N,P-CQDs was determined on a QM8000 spectrofluorometer (Horiba Scientific, USA). High performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) analysis was carried out with a Waters (Milford, MA, USA) instrument comprising a 2695 separations module capable of gradient elution and a 2996 photodiode array detector. An Agilent TC-C18 chromatographic column (4.6 mm i.d. 250 mm) packed with 5 μm octadecyl-bonded silica (C18) with 130 Å pore size (Santa Clara, CA, USA) was used. The mobile phase containing methanol and ammonium acetate solution was filtered through a 0.45 μm membrane filter before use.

N,P-CQDs were prepared from ethanediamine, citric acid and phosphoric acid by a simple hydrothermal method. In brief, citric acid (0.3 g), ethanediamine (5 mL), phosphoric acid (2.5 mL) and deionized water (10 mL) were mixed thoroughly and transferred to a 25 mL teflon-lined stain-less steel autoclave for continuous heating for 8 h in a high temperature drying oven at 180 ℃. After natural cooling to indoor temperature, the obtained reaction solution was dialyzed in a dialysis tube (molecular weight cut off: 500 Da) against deionized water for 48 h. The pure N,P-CQDs powders were obtained after freeze-drying treatment.

Miranda orange juice drink, candy, fruit jam and paprika from the local market were selected as food samples. The Miranda soft drink was treated with ultrasound for 30 min to remove CO2, and then filtered with microfilter (0.45 μm). The collected filtrate was stored in a refrigerator at 4 ℃ for further use. For candy, 5 g of crushed candy sample was weighed accurately and dissolved in 10 mL warm water. The candy sample solution was filtered by a microfilter (0.45 μm). The collected filtrate was stored in a refrigerator at 4 ℃ for further analysis. For fruit jam, 3.0 g of fruit jam was extracted with 10 mL of methanol/water (1:1, v/v) by sonication for 10 min, then centrifuged (6000 rmp) for 10 min. Extraction was repeated three times. The extracts were combined and filtered with 0.45 μm microfilter. The collected filtrate was stored in a refrigerator at 4 ℃ for further analysis. For paprika, 1 g of paprika were weighed accurately and dissolved in 10 mL methanol, then centrifuged (8000 rmp) for 6 min. The supernatant was filtered with 0.45 μm microfilter. The collected filtrate was stored in a refrigerator at 4 ℃ for further analysis. For recovery analysis, appropriate amount of new carmine solution was added to the Miranda soft drink, candy, fruit jam and paprika sample filtrate.

For the detection of new coccine, N,P-CQDs (0.25 mL, 10 mg/mL) was mixed with different amounts of new coccine solution and diluted with phosphate buffer solution (10 mM, pH = 6) to 3 mL. After the mixed solution was fully oscillated and incubated under indoor temperature for 15 min, the fluorescence spectra were collected at an excitation wavelength (λex) of 360 nm and the emission wavelength (λem) range of 380–700 nm. Both the emission slit and the excitation slit were set to 5 nm. Relative fluorescence intensity (F0/F, F0 and F refer to N,P-CQDs' fluorescence intensity in the absence and presence of new coccine, respectively) was calculated and used for quantitative analysis of new coccine. In order to test the specificity of N,P-CQDs, other substances such as metal ions (Fe3+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Al3+, K+, Ba2+, Ca2+ and Na+), vitamins (Vitamin B1 and Vitamin C), sugars (sucrose, glucose and lactose) and preservative (sodium metaphosphate, sodium benzoate and β-cyclodextrin) were added instead of new coccine and measured under the same operating conditions.

FTIR and XPS were applied to study the functional groups and elemental states on the surface of the N,P-CQDs. The FTIR spectrum of N,P-CQDs is illustrated by Fig. S1. The bands at 3310 and 2858 cm−1 belong to OH and CH stretching vibration, respectively. The bands at 1660, 1569, 1477 and 1203 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibrations of CO, CC, CN and CO groups, respectively. The band at 1439 cm−1 is due to the POH bending vibration of phosphate, indicating the existence of phosphate moiety on N,P-CQDs surface. Several bands at 1141, 1078, 1026, 961 and 899 cm−1 are corresponding to the stretching vibrations of PO, PO, POC, POH and PN group, respectively (Paredes, Villar-Rodil, Martínez-Alonso, & Tascón, 2008; Li et al., 2017). The band at 549 cm−1 belongs to NH stretching vibration (Parvin, & Mandal, 2017). Furthermore, Fig. S2 shows the XPS spectra of N,P-CQDs. The XPS survey spectrum of N,P-CQDs (Fig. S2A) shows four main binding energy peaks at 530, 399, 285 and 132 eV for O1s, N1s, C1s and P2p, respectively. The analysis results show that the atomic content percentages of C, N, O and P in the prepared N,P-CQDs are 57.8 %, 18.8 %,21.1 % and 2.3 %, respectively. The result indicates that the synthetic CQDs are effectively doped by N and P. In detail, the C1s high-resolution spectra of N,P-CQDs (Fig. S2B) reveals the existence of CH (283.5 eV), CC/CC (283.9 eV), CO/CN/CP (285.1 eV) and CO (286.8 eV) bonds (Li et al., 2017; Bourlinos et al., 2011). The N1s high-resolution spectra of N,P-CQDs (Fig. S2C) contains three peaks of CN (398.4 eV), NH (398.9 eV) and NP (400.3 eV) bonds (Li et al., 2016a; Li et al., 2016b; Li et al., 2017). The O1s high-resolution spectra of N,P-CQDs (Fig. S2D) exhibits three peaks of CO (529.4 eV), OH (530.5 eV) and PO (530.1 eV) bonds (Lu et al., 2017). The P2p high-resolution spectra of N,P-CQDs (Fig. S2E) can be fitted into three peaks of PN/CP (131.3 eV), PO (133.1 eV) and CP (132 eV) bonds (Li et al., 2016a; Li et al., 2016b; Li et al., 2017). The characterization results of FTIR and XPS are consistent, indicating that N,P-CQDs surface is rich in oxygen-containing groups, and N,P-CQDs are successfully doped with N and P. The doping of N and P makes N,P-CQDs have strong fluorescence. The abundant oxygen-containing groups ensure the synthesized N,P-CQDs have good water solubility, which is beneficial to the further detection of the target new coccine in the water environment.

UV–vis absorption and fluorescence measurements were performed to study N,P-CQDs' optical characters. The two absorption bands of N,P-CQDs showed by Fig. 1 A at 245 and 350 nm are attributed to the π → π* and n → π* electronic transition of CC and CN/CO bond, respectively (Lu et al., 2017). At the λex of 360 nm, N,P-CQDs shows the optimal fluorescence emission at a wavelength of 448 nm. The aqueous solution of N,P-CQDs is bright blue in UV light (365 nm) and light yellow in sunlight (Inset of Fig. 1A). The synthesized N,P-CQDs have a high fluorescence quantum yield of about 43.2% with quinine sulfate as the reference substance at λex of 360 nm (Fig. S3). In addition, N,P-CQDs exhibits excitation-dependent characteristics at different λex. As shown in Fig. 1B, when the λex is changed from 300 to 450 nm, the λem of N,P-CQDs is red-shifted from 444 to 503 nm. The introduction of different functional groups on N,P-CQDs surface and the presence of different size particles in the sample are the main factors leading to the existence of multiple emission sites (Sun et al., 2006).

The size and morphology of N,P-CQDs were investigated using TEM. TEM images are displayed in Fig. 2 A, which shows the spherical shape and good dispersion of N,P-CQDs in the solution. The size distribution of N,P-CQDs is depicted in Fig. 2B by randomly counting about 100 N,P-CQDs particles from TEM image. The particle size is 1.9 to 4.4 nm, and the average particle size is 3.1 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of the N,P-CQDs to new coccine
ce:section.ce:para: For purpose of exploring the best experimental conditions for new coccine detection, the effects of critical parameters such as the amount of N,P-CQDs, pH value of the system and incubation time on relative fluorescence intensity (F0/F) were investigated in detail. Firstly, N,P-CQDs solution (10 mg/mL) in the dosage range of 0.15–0.40 mL was used to test the effect of N,P-CQDs dosage on F0/F. As illustrated by Fig. S4A, F0/F increased sharply as the dosage of N,P-CQDs increased from 0.15 to 0.25 mL, and gradually decreased when the dosage of N,P-CQDs was greater than 0.25 mL. Thus, 0.25 mL of N,P-CQDs (10 mg/mL) was selected for subsequent analyses. Then, the influence of pH value on F0/F was investigated by observing the change of F0/F with pH. As displayed in Fig. S4B, the sensor system is very sensitive to pH. When pH changed from 2 to 12, F0/F fluctuated and reached the maximum at pH 6. Hence, pH 6 was adopted for the following experiment. Finally, the effect of incubation time of N,P-CQDs and new coccine on F0/F was researched. As illustrated in Fig. S4C, F0/F gradually increased as the incubation time increased from 0 to 15 min, and basically tends to be constant with the extension of incubation time (≥15 min). So, the optimal incubation time of N,P-CQDs and new coccine was 15 min. In summary, the optimal experimental conditions for new coccine detection are 0.25 mL N,P-CQDs solution (10 mg/mL), pH 6 and incubation time of 15 min.

The effect of increasing the concentration of new coccine on N,P-CQDs' fluorescence intensity was studied under the optimal experimental conditions and shown in Fig. 3 A. As the concentration of new coccine increases, N,P-CQDs' fluorescence intensity gradually weakens, which indicates that new coccine has a significant quenching effect on N,P-CQDs' fluorescence. There are two good linear relationships between F0/F and new coccine in the concentration range of 0.2–100 and 100–200 μM, respectively. As shown by Fig. 3B-C, the fitted linear regression equations are F0/F = 0.0173[C] + 1 and F0/F = 0.0459[C] − 1.957 with the correlation coefficients (R2) of 0.9959 and 0.9911, respectively. The limit of detection (LOD) is calculated by LOD = 3 s/m (s is the standard deviation of 10 blank measurements, m is the slope of the calibration curve), which are 24.8 and 9.4 nM, respectively.

The linearity and LOD of the proposed N,P-CQDs-based fluorescence method were compared with other reported methods. As displayed by Table S1, the sensitivity of the N,P-CQDs-based fluorescence method proposed in this research is much higher than that of HPLC, HPIC, CE and SERS methods previously reported. In addition, our proposed N,P-CQDs-based fluorescence method has the attractive advantages of simplicity, fast response, low cost, and good selectivity, which undoubtedly provides an innovative and efficient method for detecting new coccine.

For the reproducibility evaluation, we performed five repeated analyses on new coccine content equal to 10, 80 and 160 μM. As shown by Table S2, the relative standard deviation (RSD) obtained were 2.7 %, 2.5 % and 1.1 %, respectively, indicating that the proposed fluorescence method has high reproducibility.

In order to clarify the fluorescence quenching mechanism, related experiments were carried out. The UV absorption of new coccine and the fluorescence spectrum of N,P-CQDs were recorded and illustrated by Fig. S5A. The fluorescence excitation and emission spectrum of N,P-CQDs were obviously overlapped with the UV absorption peak of new coccine. IFE or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) is considered to be the main fluorescence quenching mechanism (Liu et al., 2017). Usually, FRET is accompanied by a shortening of the donor fluorescence lifetime, while IFE does not involve the change of the donor fluorescence lifetime (Fan et al., 2017). For FRET, vibrational collisions cause non-radiative energy transfer, and the distance is limited to 10 nm, which shortens the fluorescence lifetime of the donor (Mu et al., 2021). IFE is the attenuation or absorption of emitted radiation by the quencher of the excitation beam in the solution (Gauthler, Shane, Guerln, Seltz, & Grant, 1986). Therefore, it does not change the fluorescence lifetime of the donor. To further clarify the exact fluorescence quenching mechanism, N,P-CQDs' fluorescence lifetimes with and without new coccine were investigated. As shown in Fig. S5B, N,P-CQDs' fluorescence lifetime was 12.35 and 12.36 ns before and after the addition of new coccine, respectively, which indicates that the main quenching mechanism is IFE rather than FRET.

By studying the fluorescence response of the N,P-CQDs to new coccine and other interfering substances that may be present in the real samples, such as common metal ions (Na+, Ca2+, Ba2+, K+, Al3+, Mg2+, Zn2+ and Fe3+), sugars (lactose, glucose and sucrose), vitamins (vitamin C and vitamin B1) and preservatives (β-cyclodextrin, sodium benzoate and sodium metaphosphate), the selectivity of N,P-CQDs-based fluorescent nanosensor was investigated. As depicted by Fig. 4 , it is obvious that only new coccine can effectively quench N,P-CQDs' fluorescence, while other possible interfering substances do not cause significant changes in the N,P-CQDs' fluorescence, which indicates that the selectivity of N,P-CQDs-based fluorescent nanosensor to new coccine is higher than other potential interferences. From the quenching mechanism, the fluorescence of N,P-CQDs quenched by new coccine via IFE. IFE is a radiation energy conversion process, which is based on absorption of the excitation or emission spectra of the fluorophores by absorbers. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of interfering substances and new coccine are displayed in Fig. S6. Obviously, only the UV–vis absorption spectrum of new coccine have the significant overlap with the emission spectrum of N,P-CQDs, resulting in the significant quenching of the fluorescence of N,P-CQDs by the new coccine, thus enhance the specific recognition of N,P-CQDs to new coccine. Therefore, our prepared N,P-CQDs could be used for the construction of highly selective sensor for the detection of new coccine in real food samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of new coccine in actual food samples
ce:section.ce:para: The proposed N,P-CQDs-based fluorescent nanosensor was utilized for detecting new coccine in Mirinda soft drink, candy, fruit jam and paprika samples. Because of the excellent selectivity and high sensitivity of this fluorescence sensor, the complex food samples can be directly studied by simple sample pretreatment. The analytical results are shown by Table 1 . The high recovery values were obtained in the range of 97.5–104 %, and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) was 1.2–4.3 % (n = 5). Meanwhile, the detection of new coccine was also implemented by HPLC. The HPLC method shows a good linearity in the concentration range of 0.5–100 μM (Fig. S7). The results of HPLC determination of new coccine in food samples are also listed in Table 1 with recoveries of 97.8–103 % and RSDs of 0.8–3.6 %. There is a good agreement are observed between the results achieved by the two methods. However, compared with traditional HPLC method, the proposed N,P-CQDs-based fluorescent nanosensor is more economical, simpler and faster. The results further reveal that our developed sensor based on N,P-CQDs has good accuracy, reproducibility, practicability and reliability, and can be satisfactorily employed for detecting new coccine in actual food samples.

In this work, an original fluorescent nanosensor based on N,P-CQDs was established for ultra-sensitive detection of new coccine for the first time. N,P-CQDs were prepared via a simple one-pot hydrothermal method with citric acid, phosphoric acid and ethanediamine as carbon, phosphorus and nitrogen sources, respectively. The synthesized N,P-CQDs possess superior water-solubility and strong emission fluorescence. The fluorescence quantum yield is up to 43.2%. The synthesized N,P-CQDs are used as fluorescent nanosensor for the monitoring of new coccine, showing good selectivity, ultra-high sensitivity and anti-interference ability with a wide linear range (0.2–100 and 100–200 μM) and low detection limit (24.8 and 9.4 nM). N,P-CQDs' fluorescence was quenched by new coccine through IFE. The developed fluorescent nanosensor based on N,P-CQDs was successfully used for detecting new coccine in actual food samples with fulfilling results. The recoveries are 97.5–104 % with RSDs ≤ 4.3 %. The proposed fluorescent nanosensor based on N,P-CQDs has numerous advantages, such as easy fabrication, label free, no complicated pretreatment, mild detection condition, environment-friendly, stable, low cost, fast response, high sensitivity, simple operation, good selectivity and reliable results. It is expected to have a wide application prospect for new coccine detection in fields such as food safety and food management.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial supports from Applied Basic Research Project Fund of Shanxi Province (201901D211434) and the Doctoral Science Research Fund of Shanxi Datong University (2014-B-13 and 2016-B-13) are gratefully acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2021.130829.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
